About

Built for the hard problems
aerospace throws at you.

I’m John Li, a Mechanical Engineering graduate from Stony Brook University (May 2026) specializing in Structures & Solid Mechanics, with a 3.8 GPA, Dean’s List honors, and University Scholar distinction (top 8%). I’m continuing at Carnegie Mellon University for an MS in Mechanical Engineering, concentrating in Design and Manufacturing.

My experience covers the full spectrum of aerospace-adjacent engineering — from FEA-validated structural analysis for nuclear-adjacent lab facilities at Brookhaven National Laboratory, to NASA-funded composite materials research, to leading a multi-team rocket program through design, fabrication, and competition launch.

I’m drawn to companies like SpaceX and Blue Origin because the engineering is real, the stakes are high, and iteration speed matters. I thrive in environments where you design it, build it, test it, and fix it fast.

💻

Programming

MATLAB, Arduino, LabVIEW, circuit analysis & building, embedded electronics, SD card data acquisition.

Leadership

Led 25+ member IT team and 15+ member rocket subteams. PDR through launch, design reviews, safety compliance.

Projects

Engineering in action.

01

Battle of the Rockets — Two-Vehicle Campaign

Stony Brook University · Vehicle & Propulsion Subteam Lead · Senior Design MEC 440

Led design, fabrication, and launch of two recoverable high-power competition rockets for the 2026 FGE Battle of the Rockets in Culpeper, VA. Mars Rover vehicle achieved 1,459 ft apogee with successful rover deployment; Altitude vehicle reached 758 ft targeting 801 ft. Both vehicles recovered 100% intact at 12 ft/s and 19 ft/s descent rates. Overcame multiple engineering failures including Blue Tube warping diagnosis and a novel igniter slippage failure — diagnosed and resolved each before competition day.

SolidWorksRockSimMATLABCFDAvionicsBlack Powder EjectionPhenolic AirframeEasyMini
02

SRF Testing Facility Structural Analysis

Brookhaven National Laboratory · Mechanical Engineering Intern

Assessed structural integrity of aged high-density radiation shielding concrete beams for a new EIC superconducting RF cryomodule test facility requiring 48" minimum shielding. Conducted field surveys, modeled in PTC Creo, ran ANSYS FEA, and validated with hand calculations. Determined beams had insufficient safety margin under stacked loading — designed and implemented a beam shimming solution.

ANSYS FEAPTC CreoStructural AssessmentSafety Margin Analysis
03

EIC RF Spring Finger Thermal Test Rig

Brookhaven National Laboratory · Mechanical Engineering Intern

Designed and built a thermal characterization rig for RF-shielded bellows components operating from room temperature to 4 K (−452°F) for the Electron Ion Collider. Engineered an end cap and fixture for a 591 MHz FPC SRF component. Used additive manufacturing and embedded Arduino electronics to measure contact resistance. Analyzed data to validate component performance boundaries under simulated operational conditions.

Thermal AnalysisArduinoStrain GaugesSRF ComponentsCryogenicsAdditive Mfg.

Research

Advancing materials science.

Composites · NASA Space Grant · Dr. Kedar Kirane · SBU Materials & Mechanics Lab

Compressive Failure of Woven FRP Composites Under Open Hole Compression

Investigating how hole size affects compressive strength in woven fiber-reinforced polymer laminates used in aerospace, automotive, and naval structures. ASTM D6484 OHC tests on hole sizes 3–6 mm reveal fracture process zone (FPZ) effects that plateau strength beyond ~6 mm. Specimens cut to half standard length (150 mm) to maximize panel yield; holes drilled rather than waterjet cut for circular geometry. Sub-scale specimens converged to 42–46 MPa first-damage gross stress regardless of width — indicating a load-introduction-limited failure mode. Ongoing work isolating specimen length, grip clamping, and hole machining variables.

ASTM D6484OHC TestingWoven FRP LaminatesShimadzu UTMOptical MicrographyFPZ AnalysisHigh-Speed Imaging
